title: Central Expansion with d4
overview: White prepares c3 and d4 to challenge Black's center. Once d4 is played, the position opens and White's better-developed pieces gain activity on the central files.

pawnBreaks.explanation: The key central break. After c3 preparation, d4 opens the center when White has superior development and piece coordination.
After d4 is established, c4 can further expand the center and restrict Black's counterplay on the queenside.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The knight reroutes to g3 to support f5 or control h5, reinforcing the kingside.
The dark-squared bishop develops to an active diagonal, pinning or pressuring f6.

endgameTransitions: Favorable pawn structure after central exchange
Bishop pair advantage in open position

title: Kingside Attack with f4
overview: White plays f4 to challenge Black's e5 pawn and open the f-file for the rook. This aggressive plan aims for a direct kingside attack.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Challenges the e5 pawn directly. If exf4, White recaptures and gains the open f-file. If e5-e4, the d4 square opens for White's pieces.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The rook lifts to the third rank to join the kingside attack via g3 or h3.

endgameTransitions: Active rooks on open files if attack doesn't mate

title: e5 Advance — Space and Attack
overview: White pushes e5 to gain space, drive back the f6 knight, and create attacking chances. The advanced pawn cramps Black's position and weakens the d6 square.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Gains space and attacks the f6 knight. After dxe5, White recaptures with piece play. If the knight retreats, White has a space advantage.
After establishing e5, f5 further clamps the kingside and threatens to open lines against Black's king.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The d4 knight jumps to f5 or e6, attacking key squares around Black's king.
The bishop controls the long diagonal after e5, pressuring Black's queenside.

endgameTransitions: Superior minor piece if Black's bad bishop remains

title: f4 Kingside Attack
overview: White plays f4 followed by g4-g5 to launch a pawn storm against Black's kingside. This is the classic English Attack plan in the Najdorf.

pawnBreaks.explanation: After f4, the g4-g5 advance drives away the f6 knight and opens lines against Black's king. Combined with Qd2 and O-O-O, this creates a fierce attack.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The queen on d2 supports O-O-O and can swing to the kingside via h6 or g5.
Castling queenside gets the king safe and connects the rooks for the kingside pawn storm.

strategicThemes: Piece sacrifice on g5 or f5
endgameTransitions: Rarely reaches endgame — attack decides the game

title: Kingside Pawn Storm
overview: In the French Advance variation, White pushes f4-f5 to attack Black's pawn chain and open lines against the king. The space advantage supports piece infiltration.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Supports the e5 pawn and prepares f5 to attack Black's e6 base. This is the classic plan against the French pawn chain.
Attacks the base of the chain at e6. After exf5, White gets the e-file and f-file open for an attack.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The knight aims for f5 where it attacks e7 and supports the pawn advance.

title: Queenside Space with c3 and a3
overview: White maintains the pawn chain with c3 and builds queenside space with a3 and b4, restricting Black's counterplay while keeping the center solid.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Prepares b4 to gain queenside space and restrict Black's c5 break.
Gains queenside space and creates a passed pawn possibility. If cxb4 axb4, White's pawn majority advances.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The knight goes to c5 via b3, reaching a powerful outpost in Black's position.

title: e5 Space Advantage
overview: In the Advance Caro-Kann, White pushes e5 to gain space and restrict Black's pieces. The plan involves building on the kingside while Black struggles with the cramped position.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Gains central space and restricts Black's f6 knight development. White's plan is to use the space advantage for a kingside attack.
Supports e5 and prepares f5 to further cramp Black. This solidifies White's space advantage.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: Bd3 challenges Black's light-squared bishop, which is Black's best piece in the Caro-Kann.

endgameTransitions: Better minor pieces in closed positions

title: Queenside Minority Attack
overview: After exchanging on d5, White advances b4-b5 to undermine Black's queenside pawns. This creates weak pawns on c6 or an isolated c-pawn that White can target.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Begins the minority attack. The plan is b4-b5 to force cxb5, creating a weak backward c-pawn or isolated a-pawn for Black.
The key break. After cxb5, Black has a backward c-pawn. After c5, White has a protected passed pawn or targets b5 and d5.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The rook supports the b-pawn's advance and later pressures the b-file.
The queen supports the queenside pressure and eyes the weak c6/a7 pawns.

endgameTransitions: Rook endgame with better pawn structure

title: Minority Attack with b4-b5
overview: The classic QGD plan for White. Advance the b-pawn to b5 to attack Black's queenside pawn structure, creating a weak c6 pawn or isolated a-pawn.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Prepares b5 to attack Black's c6 pawn. This is the fundamental minority attack idea in the QGD Exchange.
Forces Black to make a structural concession. After cxb5, the c-pawn becomes weak. After c5, White blockades on d4.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: Rooks double or triple on the b-file to maximize pressure after b5 exchange.
After cxd5 exchange, Ba3 targets the dark squares and restricts Black's bishop.

endgameTransitions: Winning the weak c-pawn in endgame
Superior rook activity on open files

title: Central Break with e4
overview: White prepares e3-e4 to challenge Black's d5 pawn directly. This opens the center and activates White's pieces, especially the light-squared bishop.

pawnBreaks.explanation: The direct central challenge. After dxe4, Nxe4 gives White active centralized pieces. If Black doesn't take, tension favors White.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: After e4 and the center opens, Bd3 targets h7 and supports the kingside attack.
Ne5 is a powerful outpost controlling d7, f7, and supporting the e4 push.

title: f5 Pawn Storm
overview: Black plays f5 to attack White's e4 pawn and open the f-file. This is the classic King's Indian kingside counter-attack.

pawnBreaks.explanation: The signature KID break. Attacks e4 and opens the f-file for the rook. After exf5 gxf5, Black has a half-open g-file and active pieces.
After f5, g5-g4 drives away the f3 knight and continues the kingside pawn storm.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The knight redeploys to f4, a powerful outpost supporting the kingside attack.
After the f-file opens, rooks double on the g-file or f-file for maximum pressure.

endgameTransitions: Rarely reaches endgame — attack is decisive

title: e5 Central Break
overview: Black pushes e5 to challenge White's center directly. After d5, the position closes and both sides attack on opposite flanks.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Challenges the d4 pawn. If d5, the center closes and Black attacks kingside with f5 while White expands queenside.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: After d5 closes the center, the knight retreats then maneuvers to d4 or c5.

endgameTransitions: Blocked center leads to complex maneuvering endgames

title: d4 Central Transposition
overview: White plays d4 to transpose into Queen's Gambit structures with an extra tempo or flexible move order. This gives White central control.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Establishes a classical center. The c4+d4 pawn duo controls the center and can lead to QGD, Nimzo, or Catalan setups.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The Catalan-style fianchetto gives the bishop a powerful long diagonal controlling the center.

title: b4 Queenside Expansion
overview: White expands on the queenside with b4, gaining space and opening lines for piece play. This is the Symmetrical English plan.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Challenges Black's c5 pawn and gains queenside space. After cxb4, Nxb4 or axb4 opens the a-file.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The rook supports the b-pawn advance and pressures the b-file after exchanges.

title: d4 Central Break
overview: White prepares d4 to open the center, leveraging better piece coordination. This is the main plan in the Closed Ruy Lopez.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Opens the center when White's pieces are better placed. The bishop on b3 becomes active and the center opens for White's rooks.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The Ruy Lopez knight maneuver. The knight reroutes to f5, a dominant outpost threatening Black's king.
The bishop on b3 pressures f7 and supports the d5 advance.

title: e4 Central Push
overview: White prepares e3-e4 to challenge Black's d5 pawn and activate the light-squared bishop. This transforms the quiet London into an active game.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Challenges d5 directly. After dxe4, Nxe4 centralizes the knight. If Black maintains d5, White has central tension.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The knight centralizes on e4 after the pawn exchange, controlling key squares.
The bishop activates on d3 targeting h7 and supporting the center.

title: c4 Queenside Play
overview: White plays c4 early to challenge d5 and gain queenside space. This can transpose into Queen's Gambit structures with the bishop already developed.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Attacks d5 and gains queenside space. The London bishop is already out, giving White a flexible position.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: Qb3 attacks b7 and d5 simultaneously, creating immediate tactical pressure.

title: h4-h5 Pawn Storm
overview: White castles queenside and launches h4-h5 against Black's fianchettoed king. Opening the h-file creates devastating threats along the h-file and g-file.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Begins the pawn storm. h4-h5 aims to open the h-file and destroy Black's kingside pawn shelter.
Forces gxh5 or g5. After gxh5, the open h-file is lethal. After g5, Black's kingside is weakened.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The bishop can go to h5 to pressure f7, or retreat to b3 for long-diagonal pressure.
After h5 is played, the rook lifts to h4 and can swing to g4 for a double attack.

strategicThemes: Piece sacrifice on h5 or g5
endgameTransitions: Usually decisive in middlegame — endgame rare

title: f4 Kingside Expansion
overview: White plays f4 to challenge e5 and open the f-file. Combined with the Bb3 pressure on f7, this creates a potent kingside attack.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Attacks e5 and opens the f-file. After exf4, Bxf4 activates the bishop and the f-file is open for the rook.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The rook is already on f1 and benefits immediately from the opened f-file.

endgameTransitions: Bishop pair if dark-squared bishop is traded

title: Open Center Activity
overview: In the Scotch, White opens the center immediately with d4. The resulting open position favors White's piece development and central control.

pawnBreaks.explanation: Immediately opens the center. After exd4, Nxd4 centralizes the knight and White develops rapidly.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The knight on d4 controls key squares and can jump to f5, b5, or c6.
Rapid bishop development to an active diagonal pressuring f7 or pinning the knight.

endgameTransitions: Active pieces convert to endgame advantage

title: Central e4 Break
overview: The defining strategic ambition of the Jobava London is to establish a powerful pawn on e4. White develops with Nc3, Bf4, and Bd3 before pushing e4, after which Black's position becomes cramped and White's pieces flood into active squares.

pawnBreaks.explanation: The key central break. When executed with full piece support, it grants White a lasting space advantage and activates every piece.
A preparatory pawn move that shores up the e4 advance. f3 also adds a second pawn to support the e4 chain and can later participate in a kingside advance with g4.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The c3 knight supports e4 directly and can leap to b5 to menace c7 and force the awkward Na6.
Bd3 develops to an aggressive post aimed at the kingside. After e4, it becomes a serious attacking weapon targeting h7.

strategicThemes: Bishop pair activity after central opening
endgameTransitions: Superior minor pieces persist into simplified positions
Space advantage creates targets on d6 and c7

title: Kingside Pawn Storm vs Fianchetto
overview: When Black plays g6 and Bg7, White pivots to h4-h5 to exploit the kingside. White often castles queenside and hurls pawns at the Black king, creating a sharp imbalanced game.

pawnBreaks.explanation: The engine of the kingside attack. h5 challenges the g6 pawn and either forces gxh5 (opening the g-file) or h6 (creating a hook for g4-g5).
Following h4-h5, g4-g5 drives the f6 knight from its best defensive square. Black's kingside becomes dangerously exposed.

pieceManeuvers.explanation: The knight on e5 is a powerful outpost — centralized, aggressive, and difficult to evict. It supports the kingside attack and eyes g4.
Qd2 prepares O-O-O for opposite-side castling, connects rooks, and supports h4-h5.

endgameTransitions: Open h-file becomes a highway for rook invasion
Exposed Black king faces persistent threats even in simplified positions
